Pure decision function over `DecideProviderInput` returning `ProviderDecision`
(closed `provider` symbol set: `"claude" \| "local" \| "hold"`); the wiring
layer (slice 3) reads stdout from `scripts/check-mlx-server.mjs`, parses JSON,
and threads it into `decideProvider`. `HARD_LIMIT_PATTERNS` is the public
stability contract — adding a substring is safe, removing one is breaking and
needs a rule-#9 record. Hold-state (`provider: "hold"`) is itself an escape
hatch — when both providers are unavailable, the daemon's outer loop logs and
skips the iteration rather than spawning into the void. Operator escape hatches:
`forceClaude` (env `MINSKY_LLM_PROVIDER=claude-only`) wins over everything;
`preferLocal` (env `MINSKY_LLM_PROVIDER=local-preferred`) only wins when the
probe is reachable. Pivot threshold (rule #9): if hard-limit detection produces
≥2 missed signals/week, retire the stderr-tail substring match in favour of an
exit-code allowlist; if the probe's wrapping HTTP fetch dominates daemon
iteration latency (>500ms p95), pivot to a long-lived persistent `keep-alive`
socket. | | 83 | `.github/workflows/slsa.yml` (SLSA Build Level 3 provenance

(eds.), *Site Reliability Engineering*, O'Reilly, 2016, Ch. 6 "Effective Troubleshooting" + Ch. 11 "Being On-Call" (MTTR as the SLI for self-healing systems; `detect → apply → verify` operationalises the troubleshooting loop); Basiri, A., Behnam, N., de Rooij, R., Hochstein, L., Kosewski, L., Reynolds, J., Rosenthal, C., "Chaos Engineering", *IEEE Software* 33(3) 2016 (steady-state hypothesis + fault axis + assertion — the chaos verifier follows this pattern per catalogue entry); Helland, P., "Life beyond Distributed Transactions", *CIDR* 2007 (immutable log substrate — the heal-events ledger is the immutable derived state for MTTR); Armstrong, J., *Programming Erlang*, Pragmatic Bookshelf, 2007, Ch. 4 (let-it-crash + supervisor-restart — these heals are the layer that restores invariants after the supervisor has restarted the daemon); rule #17 (proactive healing — the agent observed the symptom, the agent ran the fix, in the same iteration); rule #3 (paired tests + GWT scenarios — `user-stories/007-agent-self-heals-catalogued-failures.md` has 20 GWT scenarios, each test references one by name); rule #7 (chaos engineering — the catalogue chaos test exercises one row per automated entry); rule #8 (pattern conformance — this row); MILESTONES.md M1.13 (phase 1 of 2 — phase 2 follow-up `promote-remaining-heal-recipes` carries the ≥10-automated bar); plan: `docs/plans/agents-can-self-heal-minsky-m1-13.md` (reviewer-approved round 2); SKILL.md § 4 "Heal catalogue" (the operator-facing index of all 11 catalogued failure modes, classified by Status: 4 automated / 6 operator-recipe / 1 blocked-by-policy after this PR) | full | The heal substrate is in production-grade shape: 36 tests green (4 helpers × ~6 + ledger 6 + reporter 14 + chaos 5), all under 400ms. Each helper writes ONLY to `.minsky/`, `node_modules/`, or build artifacts (`.tsbuildinfo` files) — never to source code, never outside the worktree. Pivot threshold (rule #9): if the chaos test produces ≥1 false-positive over 30 days of CI runs (reports `healed: true` when the underlying symptom is still present), the assertion is too lenient — pivot to requiring the helper's `verify()` step to also confirm the next-iteration's daemon log line is the expected post-heal banner, not a re-occurrence of the original signal. Phase-2 reachability gap (acknowledged by reviewer round 2): the 6 operator-recipes are not yet enumerated as automation candidates; that audit lands in `promote-remaining-heal-recipes` before phase-2 implementation. |

| 90 | `.releaserc.json` + `.github/workflows/release.yml` + `CHANGELOG.md` (semantic-release-managed release pipeline — operator directive 2026-05-21 supersedes the daily-prose CHANGELOG.md format; every push to `main` with a `feat:` / `fix:` / `perf:` / `BREAKING CHANGE:` commit fires the workflow which computes the next version from conventional commits, publishes a GitHub Release with auto-generated notes, prepends a Keep-a-Changelog row to `CHANGELOG.md`, and commits the bump back via `chore(release): vX.Y.Z [skip ci]`; archived prose format frozen at `docs/CHANGELOG-narrative-history.md`) | Continuous delivery via conventional commits (Humble & Farley 2010 — CD pipeline + Beck 1999 XP — CI as the constraint enforcer applied to release cadence); Pure-function bump decision (the version bump is a referentially-transparent function of the commit types between the last tag and HEAD); Default by default (rule #11 — release automation is the default; the agent opt-out is the `docs:` / `chore:` / `refactor:` / `test:` / `build:` / `ci:` commit type or the `[skip release]` / `[skip ci]` message footer) | Humble, J., Farley, D., *Continuous Delivery*, Addison-Wesley, 2010, Ch. 4 ("Implementing a Deployment Pipeline" — every commit is a release candidate, the gate is the test suite + commit-type classification); Beck, K., *Extreme Programming Explained*, Addison-Wesley, 1999 (CI as the constraint enforcer — applied to *release* cadence not just merge cadence); Conventional Commits 1.0.0 spec (<https://www.conventionalcommits.org/en/v1.0.0/>) — the commit-type → bump mapping; Keep a Changelog v1.1.0 spec (<https://keepachangelog.com/en/1.1.0/>) — the changelog format produced; rule #1 (vision.md § 1 — don't reinvent: `semantic-release` is the battle-tested OSS substrate, ~10,000 packages in production); rule #10 (vision.md § 10 — deterministic enforcement; the bump decision is same-input-same-output, no LLM in the chain); rule #11 (vision.md § 16 — default by default; release automation is the default for "keep changelog current"); rule #17 (vision.md § 17 — proactive healing; the CHANGELOG drift observed 2026-05-21 was the trigger); `experiments/semantic-release-rollout-2026-05-21.yaml` (the rule-#9 pre-registered hypothesis + pivot to `release-please` at ≥2 incorrect-version releases or ≥1 missing CHANGELOG row in 30 days); MILESTONES.md (M1=v0.1.0, M2=v0.2.0, M3=v0.3.0, M4=v1.0.0, M5=v2.0.0 — the versioning scheme honored) | full | The `.releaserc.json` declares 5 plugins (`commit-analyzer`, `release-notes-generator`, `changelog`, `github`, `git`); plugin order matters and is fixed to the canonical pipeline (analyze → generate → write changelog → publish github release → commit + push tag). The workflow runs only on `push: branches: [main]` with `concurrency.cancel-in-progress: false` so back-to-back releases serialise. Operator escape hatches: any commit type outside `feat` / `fix` / `perf` / `revert` skips the release; or the `[skip release]` / `[skip ci]` message footer aborts the workflow's release-eligibility check. |

Milestone alignment gate` must all exist verbatim), relative-link resolution (every `[text](path)` whose path doesn't start with `http`/`mailto:`/`#` must resolve to a real file), and `vision.md § N` citation resolution (every `vision.md § N` reference must point to a real `### N.` heading in vision.md)) | Deterministic CI gate over a PR diff (rule #10); Pure-function-with-I/O-at-edge (Martin 2017 — `checkAgentsMdCoherence` is referentially transparent over `{ agentsMd, visionMd, fileExists }`; the CLI's `readFile` + `existsSync` are the I/O surfaces); paired-fixture pattern (Meszaros 2007) — every error class (`missing-section`, `broken-link`, `stale-vision-rule-ref`) is pinned by a paired test | rule #10 (vision.md § 10 — deterministic enforcement; the lint is same-input-same-output, no LLM in the chain); rule #17 (vision.md § 17 — proactive healing; the AGENTS.md drift risk was observed during the 2026-05-21 doc-unification sweep and is closed in the same session); rule #1 (vision.md § 1 — don't reinvent: `remark-validate-links` is the canonical OSS markdown-link-checker; this lint is the minimal Minsky-specific shape that also covers `vision.md § N` citations + required-section anchors — the OSS tool doesn't know about those Minsky-specific semantics); rule #3 (vision.md § 3 — test-first: 18 paired tests ship in the same PR as the lint); Meszaros, G., *xUnit Test Patterns*, Addison-Wesley, 2007 (paired-fixture pattern for parametric cases); operator directive 2026-05-21 verbatim: "Let's ensure agents.md is always updated too"; `experiments/agents-md-coherence-lint-2026-05-21.yaml` (rule-#9 pre-registered hypothesis + pivot to narrower required-section list at ≥3 false positives in 30 days) | full | At the time of merge the lint passes on AGENTS.md as-shipped: 3 required sections present, 8 relative links resolve, 3 `vision.md § N` citations resolve. The lint is run by `pnpm pre-pr-lint --stage=fast` (added to `CI_BASH_GATE_BUCKETS.mustSucceed` in `scripts/run-pre-pr-lint-stack.mjs`) and as a discrete `agents-md-coherence` job in `.github/workflows/ci.yml` with the same body shape as the sibling `no-personal-paths-in-docs` job. Pivot threshold (rule #9, per the EXPERIMENT.yaml): ≥3 false positives in 30 days → narrow required-section list to just `## Orchestrator discipline` and split the link-resolution into a paired markdown-link-checker. | 94 | `.claude/skills/competitor-research/SKILL.md` + `scripts/competitor-research-validate.mjs` + paired test `scripts/competitor-research-validate.test.mjs` — the skill takes one URL argument and walks the agent through 6 phases (identify, research, draft, validate, verify, file follow-ups) that turn an external competitor URL into a corpus entry + research file + follow-up tasks; the validator is a pure decision function over a draft JSON that pins 6 invariants (kebab-case id, vendor-exclusion, label/homepage/kind shape, published-source citation+asOf+values, local-harness citation+harnessId, metric-id catalogue membership) and is paired-test covered with 21 cases including all `EXCLUDED_VENDOR_SUBSTRINGS` matches. | Pure-function-with-I/O-at-edge (Martin 2017, *Clean Architecture* — `validateDraft({ knownMetricIds, existingCompetitorIds, allowExisting })` is referentially transparent; rule #2 (vision.md § 2 — every dep behind interface: the validator is the seam between LLM-driven research and the corpus-mutation step; the skill can't reach into competitors.ts directly, it must pass through the validator first); rule #10 (vision.md § 10 — deterministic enforcement: the validator pins 6 invariants the LLM-driven research step can't guarantee, with paired tests for every error class); operator directive 2026-05-22 (add a skill to accept a URL of competitor and do full competitor research with all metrics and tasks); Meszaros, G., *xUnit Test Patterns*, Addison-Wesley, 2007 (paired-fixture pattern); agentskills.io spec (skill shape). | full | The skill ships with 21 paired tests on the validator (all green: kebab-case rejection, 3 vendor-exclusion matches, malformed homepage / kind / citation / asOf / values, unknown metric-id, non-finite value, missing resultSource, local-harness missing harnessId, null draft, accumulated-failures draft). The skill body cites the existing artefacts it composes (competitors.ts, metrics.ts, EXCLUDED_VENDOR_SUBSTRINGS) so a future refactor of those substrates surfaces here on the next doc-coherence sweep. Pivot: if the LLM-driven research phase produces ≥2 false-positive readings in 30 days (i.e., a number that turns out to be from a non-primary source or misattributed), tighten the validator's citation field to require a structured schema (vendor + title + url + asOf + methodology) rather than a free-text string. |
| 95 | `scripts/check-corpus-freshness.mjs` + paired test `scripts/check-corpus-freshness.test.mjs` + `scripts/auto-file-corpus-refresh-tasks.mjs` + paired test `scripts/auto-file-corpus-refresh-tasks.test.mjs` + `distribution/launchd/com.minsky.corpus-refresh-check.plist` + `distribution/systemd/minsky-corpus-refresh-check.{service,timer}` + `distribution/systemd/run-corpus-refresh-check.sh` + recurring task `corpus-discover-quarterly` (TASKS.md P2) — the auto-refresh substrate that keeps the M1.10 corpus self-maintaining. `check-corpus-freshness` is a pure `extractCorpusEntries(body)` + `computeFreshness({ competitors, now, thresholds: { fresh: 90, stale: 180 } })` decision function that classifies every competitor's `asOf` reading as fresh / stale / very-stale and reports the mean age; the CLI exits 1 when ≥1 entry is very-stale (so a launchd weekly fire can trip it). `auto-file-corpus-refresh-tasks` consumes the freshness JSON and inserts `corpus-refresh-<id>` TASKS.md entries (with full ID/Tags/Milestone/Competitive-goal/Touches/Details/Hypothesis/Success/Pivot/Measurement/Anchor block) under `## P2` for every very-stale competitor; it's idempotent (skips ids already present). The launchd + systemd units fire weekly, run check → autofile in one shell pipeline, and let the tick-loop's `/next-task` then pick up the filed `corpus-refresh-*` tasks and re-invoke `/competitor-research <url> --refresh`. The `corpus-discover-quarterly` recurring task closes the loop on the LIST half (so the corpus discovers NEW vendors quarterly, not just refreshes existing readings). | Pure-function-with-I/O-at-edge (Martin 2017, *Clean Architecture* — `extractCorpusEntries` + `computeFreshness` are referentially transparent; the CLI/runner reads `competitors.ts` at the I/O boundary) + Idempotent reconciliation (Beyer et al. 2016, *Site Reliability Engineering*, ch. 17 — running the autofile twice in a row produces the same TASKS.md state) + Scheduled-fire pattern (matches `weekly-corpus-refresh` launchd + `metrics-render` launchd already in `distribution/`, so the substrate composes with the existing scheduled-fire substrate rather than inventing a new one) | rule #4 (vision.md § 4 — visible, measurable: the freshness ratio is itself a corpus-quality metric the agent can act on); rule #6 (vision.md § 6 — stay alive: without auto-refresh, the corpus snapshots vendor performance at one point in time and rots; the loop converts a one-shot benchmark into a living substrate); rule #9 (vision.md § 9 — pivot tracker required: the `corpus-discover-quarterly` task has an explicit pivot to semi-annual cadence if 2 consecutive quarters yield 0 net-new vendors, so adding marginal vendors just to hit a vanity target is forbidden); operator directive 2026-05-22 ("add a mechanism so that minsky keeps competitors list updated and competitors there too" — this row IS that mechanism); Beyer, B., et al., *Site Reliability Engineering*, O'Reilly, 2016, ch. 17 (idempotent reconciliation as the design pattern for self-healing substrate). | full | All 28 paired tests green (18 for freshness, 10 for autofile); the runner has been exercised end-to-end in `/tmp/minsky-corpus-refresh` and is idempotent (running it twice produces zero net diff). The thresholds (90 / 180 days) match `vendor-quote-freshness` semantics elsewhere in the codebase, so an operator who learns one threshold knows them all. Pivot: if 30 consecutive days of auto-fired `corpus-refresh-*` tasks accumulate without the operator running `/competitor-research <url> --refresh` to clear them, that's a signal the refresh cadence is too aggressive (or the operator dropped the workflow); switch to a 14-day backoff before re-filing the same id, and surface the unactioned-refresh count as a scoreboard metric. |

| 96 | `knip.json` + `.dependency-cruiser.cjs` + expanded `biome.json` ruleset — the lint-hardening substrate that detects dead code, circular dependencies, and bug-prone code patterns. `knip` 6.x finds unused files, exports, dependencies, and class/enum members across the entire pnpm workspace (config declares entry points = `bin/*` + `scripts/*.mjs` + workspace package `src/index.ts` + paired `*.test.*` files). `dependency-cruiser` 17.x detects circular imports, orphan modules, and forbids prod→test edges + phantom-dependency imports. The expanded biome ruleset adds 13 new rules across performance (`noAccumulatingSpread`, `noDelete`), bug-prevention (`useAwait`, `useNumberNamespace`, `useThrowOnlyError`, `useThrowNewError`, `noUselessElse`, `noParameterAssign`, `noNamespace`, `useNodejsImportProtocol`, `noUndeclaredVariables`, `noEmptyBlockStatements`) on top of the existing recommended set. Both new tools land in the `pre-pr-lint --stage=full` stack as the `knip` (5s) and `depcruise` (1.2s) steps, plus parallel CI jobs in `.github/workflows/ci.yml`; new rules are at WARN level initially with a documented ratchet path to ERROR (per rule #10) once each rule family's violation count converges to ≤5. | Static-analysis pipeline (Aho-Sethi-Ullman 1986, *Compilers* — three orthogonal analyzers running independently: biome=syntactic+stylistic, knip=reachability over the import graph, depcruise=dependency-graph + cycle detection) + Ratchet/Graduation pattern (rule #10 internal — WARN→ERROR transition gated on observed convergence) + Multi-engine lint stack (industry-standard 2026 TypeScript stack per Code With Seb, *Hardcore TypeScript + Biome + ESLint*, 2026 — biome for fast inline lint, complementary tools for what biome doesn't cover) | rule #4 (visible — every rule's violation count is a measurable scoreboard metric); rule #6 (stay alive — dead code, circular deps, and unhandled-promise bugs all degrade the daemon's MTTR); rule #10 (deterministic enforcement — every new rule is a CI gate, not an LLM advisory); operator directive 2026-05-23 ("Check all ways we can make code much more performant and bug-free with lint rules"); Aho, Sethi, Ullman, *Compilers: Principles, Techniques, and Tools*, Addison-Wesley, 1986 (dataflow analysis as the foundation of dead-code detection); knip docs at knip.dev; dependency-cruiser docs at github.com/sverweij/dependency-cruiser; Pinna et al. *Hardcore TypeScript + Biome + ESLint*, pocketarc.com/typescript, 2026 (the 2026 multi-engine lint stack pattern). | full | Initial install findings: knip surfaces 22 dead-code candidates (2 unused files, 3 unused deps, 5 unused exports, 7 unused exported types); depcruise surfaces 15 graph violations (7 circular barrel-file cycles within adapter packages, 8 orphan modules from CLI scripts the bash entry invokes); biome adds 199 new warnings (155 `useAwait` advisory + 38 `noEmptyBlockStatements` mock-impl placeholders + 6 misc) on top of 0 errors. All 53 pre-pr-lint stages green; both new CI jobs added to `.github/workflows/ci.yml` with bash-gate parity. Pivot: if a WARN-level rule's findings remain unchanged for 60 days (i.e., the corpus didn't drain), demote it to advisory-only — keeping a permanent-warn rule trains operators to ignore lint output, which is worse than removing the rule. The follow-up task `lint-warnings-drain-to-zero` tracks the 199-warning corpus's drain trajectory. |

| 97 | `competitors/README.md` — the strategic landscape synthesis. Enumerates Minsky's 6 moats (daemon-not-framework, operator-machine identity, constitution + deterministic CI, MAPE-K self-improvement, cross-repo fleet, TASKS.md as operator surface) + 5 honest gaps (headline benchmark, enterprise distribution, multi-agent ensembling — each filed in TASKS.md; graph-based time-travel + Python framework binding — rejected by design). Per-vendor research files live as `competitors/<id>.md`; the README is the synthesis layer. Cross-referenced from vision.md § "What Minsky uniquely does" and from `ARCHITECTURE.md` § "Competitive layer-by-layer". Read order for cold readers: vision.md TL;DR → vision.md § "What Minsky uniquely does" → this file. | Diátaxis four-quadrant docs pattern (Procida 2017 — this file is the *explanation* quadrant for the competitive landscape; per-vendor files are *reference*) + Moat / Differentiation Analysis (Porter 1980 — what's distinctive vs the competitive set) + Trust-but-verify discipline (rule #10 — each claim has a file-path anchor and each gap has a falsifiable task) | rule #1 (vision.md § 1 — don't reinvent: the synthesis names what competitors DO have, so future PRs don't accidentally rebuild it); rule #4 (vision.md § 4 — visible: moats + gaps are table data, not implicit knowledge); operator directive 2026-05-23 ("Do another deep dive on competitors ... Propose vision + user stories + architecture changes"); Sheremetyev, F., *Git Wasn't Designed for Agents*, Medium, 2026-05; Cognition Labs, *Enterprise Deployment Overview*, docs.devin.ai, 2026; Procida, D., *The Diátaxis Documentation Framework*, diataxis.fr. | full | The 6 moats are each anchored to a user story or substrate path; the 5 gaps are each filed as a TASKS.md task with hypothesis + success + pivot. Pivot: if 12 months pass and Minsky still has zero deployed instances outside the operator's own infrastructure, at least one of the filed gaps is the wrong gap to file. Re-evaluate which gap actually blocks adoption. |
| 98 | `user-stories/012-operator-machine-identity-moat.md` — pins moat #2 as a user story with the full 10-section template (story, acceptance criteria, metric, integration test, proof, failure modes + chaos verification, pre-registered umbrella experiment, status, pattern conformance, security & privacy). Status: 🟡 partial — the moat exists today (`spawn(agent, args, { cwd: hostDir })` in `novel/cross-repo-runner/bin/minsky-run.mjs`); integration test (`test/integration/operator-machine-identity.test.ts`) + 5-row chaos table (missing `~/.gitconfig` / stale `~/.config/gh/` / rate-limited API key / agent-spawns-docker / root-uid-refusal) are the critical path to ✅ done. The umbrella experiment measures time-to-first-operator-review for Minsky-opened PRs vs Devin-opened PRs. | Local-first software pattern (Kleppmann, Wiggins, van Hardenberg, McGranaghan, *Local-first software*, CIDR 2019, § 4 "You retain ownership and control" — agent's commits, credentials, and working tree live on the operator's machine) + User story / acceptance scenario (Cohn, *User Stories Applied*, 2004 — the 10-section template Minsky's user-stories/ directory follows) + Pin-against-regression invariant (rule #10 ratchet — `operator-machine-identity-coverage` metric is the visible regression signal) | rule #4 (visible — the moat is enforced as a measurable metric, not just documented prose); rule #7 (chaos engineering — 5 specific failure modes pinned with expected behavior + chaos test); rule #13 (security & privacy — the moat IS Minsky's security model, threat 1-5 enumerated); operator directive 2026-05-23; competitors/README.md § "What Minsky uniquely does" row 2; Sheremetyev, F., *Git Wasn't Designed for Agents*, Medium, 2026-05; Cognition Labs, *Devin Enterprise Deployment Overview*, docs.devin.ai, 2026 (the Brain + Devbox architecture this user story is the deliberate inverse of). | full | The user story ships with 5 explicit failure modes + pre-registered umbrella experiment. Pivot: if 60 days pass with no measurable review-time difference between Minsky-opened and Devin-opened PRs (≤20% delta), reframe the moat as "no credential provisioning required" rather than "faster review" — the TECHNICAL property still holds but the BUSINESS outcome wasn't what we predicted. |
| 99 | `user-stories/013-daemon-not-framework-moat.md` — pins moat #1 as a user story with the full 10-section template. Status: ✅ done — the daemon shape exists today (`bin/minsky` is a bash entry the operator runs; host repos contain zero `@minsky/*` imports). Pin-against-regression: the `framework-lines-in-host-repo` lint (`git grep -E "(from|import)\s+['\"]?@minsky/" <host-dir>/src` must return zero lines). Pre-registered umbrella experiment measures time-to-first-PR from `git clone` (target: <5 minutes across diverse host repos in TS/Python/Rust/Go). 6-row chaos table (host missing TASKS.md / malformed TASKS.md / host not a git repo / host imports @minsky / custom persona via YAML / daemon SIGKILL mid-iteration). | Daemon / Service pattern (Newman, *Building Microservices*, O'Reilly 2021, Ch. 1 — a daemon is a service the operator attaches; a framework is a library the developer wraps) + Supervision tree (Armstrong, *Programming Erlang*, 2013, Ch. 14 — launchd/systemd is the outer supervisor) + User story / acceptance scenario (Cohn 2004) | rule #1 (don't reinvent — the moat means we don't rebuild what every framework already does); rule #4 (visible — `framework-lines-in-host-repo` metric is the regression signal); rule #6 (stay alive — `daemon-sigkill-mid-iteration` chaos test pins the supervisor-restart behavior); rule #12 (scope discipline — the daemon never edits host source files outside the task's declared scope); operator directive 2026-05-23; competitors/README.md § "What Minsky uniquely does" row 1; Newman, S., *Building Microservices*, O'Reilly, 2021, Ch. 1; Armstrong, J., *Programming Erlang*, Pragmatic Bookshelf, 2013, Ch. 14. | full | The user story is the most-shipped of the moats (✅ done today). Pivot: if median time-to-first-PR exceeds 15 minutes across 5+ host repos, the daemon-not-framework moat has a hidden setup cost we haven't accounted for. Audit the install path; consider shipping `minsky bootstrap --auto`. |
| 100 | `ARCHITECTURE.md` § "Competitive layer-by-layer" — 14-row table mapping Minsky's architectural surface to each of 5 orchestrator-tier competitors (CrewAI, AutoGen, LangGraph, MetaGPT, OpenAI Agents SDK). Symbol legend: ✅ has it / 🟡 partial / ❌ doesn't have it / ⚪ rejected-by-design. The table makes the moat-shape visible architecturally — 6 ✅ Minsky-only rows (daemon, operator-machine identity, constitution+CI, MAPE-K, cross-repo fleet, TASKS.md surface) + 2 🟡 partial rows (state persistence, multi-role pipeline) + 4 ❌ gap rows (headline benchmark, enterprise distribution, multi-agent ensembling — each filed in TASKS.md) + 2 ⚪ rejected-by-design rows (graph-based time-travel, Python framework). Each ❌ row cross-references the TASKS.md task that proposes to close it; each ⚪ row cross-references vision.md § "Honest gaps" for the rejection rationale. | Trade-off matrix (Card-Mackinlay 1999 *Readings in Information Visualization*, Ch. 1 — comparative ranking across orthogonal dimensions for fast scanning) + Pattern-language documentation (Alexander 1977 *A Pattern Language* — each row IS a documented architectural pattern with explicit conformance/non-conformance) | rule #1 (don't reinvent — the table names what competitors have so future PRs don't accidentally rebuild it); rule #4 (visible — competitive position is now table data, scanable in 30 seconds); rule #15 (milestone alignment gate — ARCHITECTURE.md is one of the 7 surfaces this gate enforces); operator directive 2026-05-23; competitors/README.md § "Architectural patterns we adopt or reject"; Card, S., Mackinlay, J., Shneiderman, B., *Readings in Information Visualization: Using Vision to Think*, Morgan Kaufmann, 1999. | full | The table is the canonical "what's distinctive about Minsky's architecture vs the orchestrator tier" surface. Every ❌ row has a filed-and-tracked TASKS.md task; every ⚪ row has a vision.md § "Honest gaps" rationale. Pivot: if 4 successive PRs add ✅ rows to the table without any actual moat-strengthening (i.e., the additions are vanity table-padding), the table is being used to claim moat-coverage that doesn't exist. Re-audit the moat criteria. |

| 101 | `README.md` § "How Minsky compares to other tools" — the user-facing competitor comparison section, deliberately placed AFTER install + quickstart + benefits + how-it-works so a cold reader has the substrate context before reading the comparison. 13-row tradeoff matrix (Capability × {Minsky, OpenHands, CrewAI, Devin, Claude Code/Aider}) covering: shape, where-it-runs, credentials, coding-specific?, 24/7-unattended, cross-repo-fleet, constitutional-rules-CI, MAPE-K, operator-queue, headline-benchmark, enterprise-distribution, funding-signal, license. Followed by three short sections — "Where Minsky is uniquely strong" (5 bullets of moats), "Where Minsky has real tradeoffs" (3 bullets of honest gaps — no-headline-benchmark, single-operator-deployment-shape, coding-specific-by-design — each linking to the filed task that proposes to close it), "What we steal from each" (one-line per competitor), and a one-paragraph "if-you-want-X-pick-Y" summary. Cold-reader path: README TL;DR → How it works → How Minsky compares → Where to read next. Distinct from `competitors/README.md` (synthesis layer for landscape analysis) and ARCHITECTURE.md § "Competitive layer-by-layer" (architecture-mapping table). | Trade-off matrix (Card-Mackinlay 1999 *Readings in Information Visualization*, Ch. 1 — comparative ranking across orthogonal dimensions for fast scanning) + Diátaxis explanation quadrant (Procida 2017 — README.md is the *tutorial* quadrant for newcomers, this section is its embedded *explanation* layer) + Honest-marketing pattern (Pasquale 2015 *The Black Box Society* — claims with anchors are more persuasive than claims with marketing) | rule #4 (visible — gaps must be visible at the README level, not buried in competitor research files); rule #15 (milestone alignment gate — README.md is one of the 7 surfaces); operator directive 2026-05-22 ("Also btw it would help to have a user-facing competitors comparison maybe even somewhere down in readme that will list why minsky is better and what are real tradeoffs"); competitors/README.md § Honest gaps; competitors/openhands.md § "Why choose Minsky over OpenHands" + § "Why choose OpenHands over Minsky"; Pasquale, F., *The Black Box Society*, Harvard University Press, 2015, Ch. 1 (transparency as a competitive moat in adversarial market positioning). | full | The 13-row table + 3 tradeoff bullets + 4 steal-from-each bullets + 1-paragraph summary fits in ~80 README lines. Pivot: if operators report "the table is too long to read" (e.g., 3+ feedback items asking us to compress it), drop to 8 rows by merging similar capabilities (shape+where-it-runs into one row; constitutional+MAPE-K into one row). |

| 102 | `docs/prior-art-and-name-collisions.md` — the historical-lineage doc. Two functions in one file: (a) a disambiguation table for the 5+ unrelated projects also called "Minsky" (highperformancecoder/minsky = the economist-Hyman-Minsky modelling tool with 366★ that dominates "Minsky GitHub" searches; minskylab = a Peruvian collective; minsky.app = NeuroBridge multilingual AI; edobry/minsky = an architectural sibling using cybernetics for HITL coordination); (b) the prior-art lineage in 6 categories — Society of Mind (Marvin Minsky 1986, the namesake), MAPE-K (Kephart & Chess 2003, the control loop), VSM (Beer 1959/1972, the recursive structure), Erlang let-it-crash (Armstrong 2003, the failure model), continuous-execution daemons (Dependabot / OSS-Fuzz / Renovate / semantic-release / Mergify), and the recent LLM lineage (Reflexion / DSPy / AlphaEvolve / CrewAI / AutoGen). Linked from README's "About the name" tier-6 section. Operator entry path: cold reader who hit the wrong Minsky in a search engine + curious reader who wants the "is this a new idea?" answer. | Trade-off matrix (Card-Mackinlay 1999 — disambiguation table) + Diátaxis explanation quadrant (Procida 2017 — historical lineage as standalone reference) + Pattern-language documentation (Alexander 1977 — each row IS a named, citable pattern with file/paper anchor) | rule #1 (don't reinvent — the lineage section is the "what's already been built that we should steal from / cite / not duplicate" reference for every future feature PR); rule #5 (theoretical grounding — every architectural decision must trace to a named published pattern; this doc is the master index of those patterns); operator directive 2026-05-22 ("research what else exists with minsky word in it world and what similar implementations has anyone done in history before. Let's maybe create a doc for that"); README.md § "About the name" (links here); competitors/README.md § "What Minsky uniquely does" (cross-references the synthesis claim — "11th rung of a 70-year ladder; nothing here is new; the combination is"); Card, S., Mackinlay, J., Shneiderman, B., *Readings in Information Visualization*, 1999; Procida, D., *The Diátaxis Documentation Framework*, diataxis.fr; Alexander, C., *A Pattern Language*, Oxford University Press, 1977. | full | The doc is the canonical "is this Minsky the X Minsky?" disambiguation surface + the canonical "where did this Minsky's ideas come from?" reference. Pivot: if 90 days pass and the disambiguation table accumulates 3+ NEW Minsky-named projects we hadn't catalogued, the namespace is more crowded than expected; refactor the doc into a dedicated `docs/namespace/` directory with one file per project + a top-level index. |

When you add a new top-level artifact (file, package, interface, named
architectural decision, named process step), append a row here in the same
commit. When a deviation evolves, edit the row's notes column. When an artifact
is removed, remove its row.

## Cost schedule

Per-rule cost weights consumed by `@minsky/mape-k-loop`'s `analyze` phase
(Goldratt 1984 Theory of Constraints —
`topConstraint = max(violationCount × cost)`). Sourced from this section by
`parseCostSchedule(visionMdContent)` (`novel/mape-k-loop/src/cost-schedule.ts`);
the CLI wrapper around `tick(...)` reads `vision.md` once at startup and threads
the result through `analyze({ snapshot, costs })`. Rules absent from this table
fall back to `DEFAULT_RULE_COST = 1` (rule #7 graceful-degrade). v0 defaults;
revisit at the next quarterly review (`review-q3-2026`). Anchors: rule #4
(vision.md § 4 — every constant in source); Goldratt, *The Goal*, North River
Press, 1984.

| Rule ID   | Cost weight | Rationale                                                        |
| --------- | ----------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- |
| rule-9    | 100         | Iron rule; pre-registration violation invalidates the experiment |
| rule-7    | 50          | Chaos failures = unobserved blast radius                         |
| rule-typo | 1           | Cosmetic only; high frequency, low semantic cost                 |

## Theoretical foundations

Each layer in the architecture maps to a published, decades-tested pattern.

| Pattern | Source | Maps to |
|---|---|---|
| Society of Mind | Marvin Minsky 1986 | The persona/agent layer (provided by the cloud agent) |
| Viable System Model | Stafford Beer 1972 | The project's vertical layering — Operations → Coordination → Control → Intelligence → Identity |
| Actor Model | Hewitt, Bishop, Steiger 1973 | Persona-to-persona handoffs through the shared blackboard |
| OTP Supervision Trees | Armstrong 2007 | How Minsky recovers from any failure — supervisor restart policy |
| MAPE-K Loop (Autonomic Computing) | Kephart & Chess, IEEE Computer 2003 | The autonomic-manager loop (Monitor → Analyze → Plan → Execute over Knowledge) |
| OODA Loop | John Boyd | The per-tick decision cycle inside each persona |
| PDCA Cycle | Shewhart / Deming | Per-task structure |
| Theory of Constraints | Goldratt | The autonomic manager's attention discipline — one bottleneck at a time |
| Belief-Desire-Intention | Bratman 1987 | Persona file structure |
| Blackboard Architecture | Hayes-Roth 1985 | TASKS.md + handoffs/ folder |
| Error Budgets | Beyer SRE 2016 | Token budget = error budget |
| Autopoiesis | Maturana & Varela 1980 | The long-term ambition — the manager maintains the personas that maintain the codebase |
| Strange Loops | Hofstadter 1979 | The autonomic manager observing its own performance |
| DSPy / programming-not-prompting | Khattab 2023 | How the autonomic manager improves persona prompts over time |
| Runtime Verification | Havelund & Goldberg 2008 | Runtime spec monitoring — `vision.md` is the spec; the deterministic linters (rule #10) are the monitors |
| Calm Technology / Glanceable Display | Weiser & Brown 1995; Card & Mackinlay 1999 | The wrist surface — three numbers, no chrome |
| USE / RED Methods | Brendan Gregg; Tom Wilkie | Dashboard methodology |

These are organs of one body, not a checklist. They cohere.

## Glossary — every term has a CS anchor

This table operationalizes constitutional principle 5. Every word Minsky
introduces — whether a metaphor for narrative ease or a technical label — points
back to a published computer-science / control-theory / formal-methods source.
**Code, package names, file paths, test names, and CLI flags use the precise
term in the right column.** Prose may use the metaphor in the left column,
provided the precise term has been introduced once on the same page.

Default discipline: **use the right-column term directly**. The left-column
metaphor is permitted only when (a) it's a primary literature term in its own
right (e.g., "society of minds" — Minsky 1986; "strange loop" — Hofstadter 1979)
or (b) the metaphor adds narrative weight that the precise term loses
(currently: "constitution" for the project-spec-as-authority framing). Every
coined word that has a precise alternative in the right column is **retired**
for new prose unless the row notes otherwise — see "Retired terms" below the
table.

| Term in use                                                                                              | Precise / canonical anchor                       | Source                                                                                                                                                             |
| -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| MAPE-K loop / autonomic manager                                                                          | autonomic computing reference architecture       | Kephart & Chess, "The Vision of Autonomic Computing", *IEEE Computer* 2003                                                                                         |
| Manager agent                                                                                            | manager / dispatcher role in multi-agent systems | Wooldridge, *An Introduction to MultiAgent Systems* (2009); CrewAI's own term                                                                                      |
| Constitution / constitutional rule N                                                                     | behavioral specification / invariants            | Lamport, "Specifying Concurrent Program Modules", *TOPLAS* 1983                                                                                                    |
| Specification drift (system) / concept drift (data)                                                      | standard ML / runtime-verification term          | Widmer & Kubat, "Learning in the Presence of Concept Drift", *Machine Learning* 1996                                                                               |
| Glanceable / ambient display ("Watch surface" remains as the *specific affordance* name)                 | calm-technology display                          | Card & Mackinlay 1999; Weiser & Brown, "Calm Technology" 1995                                                                                                      |
| Scheduler iteration ("tick" is acceptable shorthand — already a real-time-systems term)                  | control-loop period                              | Liu, *Real-Time Systems* (2000)                                                                                                                                    |
| Lease ("claim" remains in the tasks.md spec as the verb; the underlying mechanism is a lease)            | mutual exclusion with timeout                    | Gray & Cheriton, "Leases", *SOSP* 1989                                                                                                                             |
| Handoff (industry-standard in agentic systems; Anthropic Agent Teams uses it explicitly)                 | actor message-passing with continuation          | Hewitt, Bishop, Steiger 1973                                                                                                                                       |
| Specialist agent / persona                                                                               | role-based agent                                 | Wooldridge 2009                                                                                                                                                    |
| Society of minds                                                                                         | (primary literature term)                        | Marvin Minsky, *The Society of Mind* (1986)                                                                                                                        |
| Strange loop                                                                                             | (primary literature term)                        | Hofstadter, *Gödel, Escher, Bach* (1979)                                                                                                                           |
| Watchdog (in `claude-budget-guard`)                                                                      | (already a precise CS term)                      | Watchdog timer, hardware / OS literature                                                                                                                           |
| Inner loop / outer loop                                                                                  | (already precise)                                | Optimization & control systems                                                                                                                                     |
| Bottleneck                                                                                               | (already precise)                                | Goldratt TOC (cited above)                                                                                                                                         |
| Error budget                                                                                             | (already precise)                                | Beyer et al., *Site Reliability Engineering* (Google, 2016)                                                                                                        |
| Failure-mode response: `loud-crash-supervisor-restart` / `circuit-break-and-notify` / `graceful-degrade` | (labels for established patterns)                | Armstrong, *Programming Erlang* 2007 (let-it-crash); Nygard, *Release It!* 2007 (circuit breaker); AWS Well-Architected, Reliability pillar (graceful degradation) |
| Fault axis                                                                                               | (chaos-engineering term)                         | Basiri et al., "Principles of Chaos Engineering", *IEEE Software* 2016                                                                                             |
| Steady-state hypothesis                                                                                  | (chaos-engineering term)                         | Basiri et al. 2016 (above)                                                                                                                                         |
| Blast radius                                                                                             | (established term)                               | AWS Well-Architected, Reliability pillar; Beyer et al., *SRE* Ch. 17 2016                                                                                          |
| Operator escape hatch / kill switch                                                                      | (established term)                               | Beyer et al., *SRE* Ch. 17 2016                                                                                                                                    |
| `scope-leak`                                                                                             | scope-creep verdict per rule #12                 | rule #12 (vision.md § "Constitutional rules") — agent touched files outside the declared `Files:` set; deterministic gate `scripts/check-rule-12-scope-discipline.mjs` |
| `spawn-failed`                                                                                           | infrastructural-failure verdict                  | rule #1 (loud-crash > silent failure) + Armstrong 2007 (let-it-crash) — agent process couldn't start / was killed by signal / exited without producing output       |
| `filed-blocked-because`                                                                                  | task-status verb required by rule #17            | rule #17 (vision.md § 17 — proactive healing); status messages must carry an active verb (`fixed`, `patched`, `rolled out`, or `filed-blocked-because`)             |
| `otel-no-pii-in-spans`                                                                                   | PII filter on OpenTelemetry attributes           | rule #13 minimum-bar item #2 (vision.md § 13); enforced by `scripts/check-otel-no-pii.mjs`; OpenTelemetry semantic conventions / data classification 2025          |

### Retired terms (do not use in new prose)

These coined terms appeared in earlier drafts. Use the canonical term in the
right column instead. New PRs that re-introduce a retired term are flagged in
review.

| Retired                               | Use instead                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| CTO loop / CTO meta-loop              | MAPE-K loop (and *autonomic manager* for the component)                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| TPM (technical-program-manager agent) | manager agent                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
| Constitutional review                 | runtime specification monitoring — deterministic share = `scripts/check-rule-{1..7}-*.mjs` + `scripts/check-pattern-index.mjs` + `scripts/check-pr-self-grade.mjs`; advisory share = `novel/spec-monitor/` Skill (advisory-only, never gates CI) |
| Drift (standalone)                    | specification drift (system behavior) or concept drift (data)                                                                                                                                                                                    |

When you introduce a new word in any Minsky doc, **add a row to the in-use table
in the same commit** — or, preferably, remove the new word and use an existing
row's right-column term.

## Success criteria

These metrics are tracked on the dashboard from day one. Each has a
corresponding integration test in `user-stories/`. Targets are starting points;
the MAPE-K loop adjusts them based on observed reality.

Per constitutional rule #9, every row carries a **success threshold**, a **pivot
threshold** (below which the *approach* is reconsidered), and a **measurement
method** that is an exact runnable command — reproducible by an outside observer
with no manual steps. Where the prerequisite system isn't built yet, the command
names the future query and tags it `<TBD-AFTER: <task-id>>` linking to the task
that lands it.

| #   | Metric                                                               | Success threshold                                                                           | Pivot threshold                                                                                  | Measurement method (automated)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       | Literature anchor                                                                                                                                                                                                           |
| --- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| 1   | Loop uptime, 30 / 90 / 365 d                                         | 99 % / 97 % / 95 %                                                                          | <90 % over 30 d → reconsider supervisor design                                                   | `systemctl --user is-active minsky-tick-loop && journalctl --user -u minsky-tick-loop --since="30 days ago" -o json \| node scripts/uptime.mjs` ⟨TBD-AFTER: supervisor-setup⟩                                                                                                        | Beyer et al., *SRE* 2016, Ch. 4 (SLI / SLO)                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| 2   | Tokens per closed user-story                                         | Decreasing trend month-over-month (≥5 % MoM)                                                | Flat or rising for 3 consecutive months → MAPE-K loop isn't helping; pivot the autonomic manager | OTEL trace query: `sum(token_count{event="user_story.complete"}[30d]) / count(span{name="user_story.complete"}[30d])` ⟨TBD-AFTER: observability-adapter-v0⟩                                                                                                                          | Goldratt TOC (improving the constraint should move this metric)                                                                                                                                                             |
| 3   | Specification alignment (deterministic-linter green ratio over 30 d) | ≥95 % of CI runs on `main` are green across the rule-#10 lint set                           | <85 % over 7 d → spec is wrong OR system is misaligned; trigger spec audit                       | `gh run list --workflow ci.yml --branch main --status completed --created ">=$(date -v-30d +%Y-%m-%d)" --limit 1000 --json conclusion --jq '([.[] \| select(.conclusion=="success")] \| length) / (length \| if . == 0 then 1 else . end)'` ⟨TBD-AFTER: rule-#10 lint set finalised⟩ | Havelund & Goldberg, "Verify Your Runs", *VSTTE* 2008 (operationalised per rule #10 — the deterministic linters at rows 32–37 are the load-bearing monitors; advisory-only `novel/spec-monitor/` does not feed this metric) |
| 4   | Self-improvement velocity                                            | ≥4 prompt rollouts / month with sustained gain (≥10 %, p < 0.05, 7 d post-rollout) after Q1 | <2 / month sustained 3 months → MAPE-K design or DSPy choice is wrong; pivot                     | `git log --grep='mape-k rollout' constraints.md --since="30 days ago" \| wc -l` plus DSPy A/B export ⟨TBD-AFTER: mape-k-loop-v0⟩                                                                                                                                                     | Khattab DSPy 2023; Kohavi *Trustworthy* 2020 (statistical rigour)                                                                                                                                                           |
| 5   | Mean time to recovery (MTTR)                                         | <5 min p95 from process death to next claim                                                 | p95 >10 min sustained 7 d → supervisor backoff or claim-resume is wrong                          | OTEL: `histogram_quantile(0.95, supervisor_restart_to_claim_latency_seconds[7d])` ⟨TBD-AFTER: observability-adapter-v0⟩                                                                                                                                                              | Forsgren et al., *Accelerate* 2018 (DORA MTTR)                                                                                                                                                                              |
| 6   | Wrist dwell (inverted)                                               | ≤60 s / day                                                                                 | >120 s / day for 14 d → surface is too informative or system is too unhealthy; redesign          | `count(http_get_total{path="/watch.json"}[1d]) * estimated_dwell_seconds_per_request` (constant ≈ 2 s) ⟨TBD-AFTER: dashboard-web-v0⟩                                                                                                                                                 | Card & Mackinlay 1999; Weiser & Brown 1995 (calm tech: dwell as friction)                                                                                                                                                   |
| 7   | Extraction count                                                     | ≥4 OSS repos extracted by month 6                                                           | <2 by month 4 → re-evaluate extraction policy / scope                                            | `gh repo list fyodoriv --json name,createdAt,description --jq '[.[] \| select(.description \| test("@minsky\|claude-")) ] \| length'`                                                                                                                                                | rule #1 (don't reinvent the wheel) — extraction is the operationalisation                                                                                                                                                   |
| 8   | Dependency interface coverage                                        | 100 % of named deps behind adapter                                                          | ≥1 unhidden dep persisting >1 sprint → fix with adapter wrap or task                             | `node scripts/check-rule-2-dep-coverage.mjs` (greps `novel/**/*.ts` excluding `novel/adapters/**` for vendor imports listed in ARCHITECTURE.md dependency table; exit 1 on any hit)                                                                                                  | rule #2 (every dep behind interface)                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| 9   | Token-budget honoring                                                | 0 hard 429 / week sustained 30 d                                                            | ≥1 / week sustained 4 weeks → budget-guard logic is broken; pivot                                | OTEL counter: `sum(rate(claude_code_api_errors_total{status="429"}[7d]))` ⟨TBD-AFTER: observability-adapter-v0 + budget-guard-v0⟩                                                                                                                                                    | Beyer SRE 2016 (error budget)                                                                                                                                                                                               |
| 10  | Task throughput                                                      | Sustained tasks / day at observed budget (≥1 / day at green budget)                         | <1 / day for 14 d at green budget → bottleneck elsewhere; analyse via TOC                        | `git log --since="30 days ago" --oneline --grep='^feat\|^fix\|^docs\|^chore' \| wc -l` divided by 30 (close-task removal commits)                                                                                                                                                    | Goldratt TOC (throughput as the goal of any system)                                                                                                                                                                         |

Tooling note: GrowthBook (open-source feature flags + A/B testing, MIT) is the
planned `Experiment` adapter for system-level rollouts behind these metrics; see
`research.md` § "Hypothesis-driven development tooling".

## License & openness

Code: MIT for `novel/*`, the dependencies' own licenses for everything else. Documentation: CC-BY-4.0. The novel layers are extractable as standalone OSS repos from day one; the integration distribution is what makes them cohere.

## North star

A developer attaches Minsky to any git repo with one command, runs it with one command, and it measurably outperforms doing the same work manually or with any competing autonomous coding tool — with proof. The roadmap to get there: [`MILESTONES.md`](MILESTONES.md).
